name: kettlebell double jerk
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand at shoulder height.
Bend your knees slightly and engage your core.
Press the kettlebells overhead, fully extending your arms.
Bend your knees and quickly drop into a partial squat.
Explosively extend your hips and knees, driving the kettlebells overhead.
Lock out your arms and catch the kettlebells overhead with your knees slightly bent.
Stand up straight and return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand at shoulder height. Bend your knees slightly and engage your core. Press the kettlebells overhead, fully extending your arms. Bend your knees and quickly drop into a partial squat. Explosively extend your hips and knees, driving the kettlebells overhead. Lock out your arms and catch the kettlebells overhead with your knees slightly bent. Stand up straight and return to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
